summary: Gruber responds to Donald Trump's campaign promise to bring Apple manufacturing back to the U.S. by systematically dismantling the argument. He cites Tim Cook's explanation that China's advantage isn't just wages but vocational skills and scale — the U.S. simply lacks the trained workforce and facilities to assemble nearly a million iPhones per day. Gruber argues that even if Apple paid good wages, there are no U.S. facilities capable of operating at that scale, and Apple strategically avoids owning assembly plants. The only path to U.S. manufacturing would be full automation, which by definition wouldn't create jobs. He concludes with a footnote wondering whether a hypothetical Apple car might be different, given the U.S.'s existing automotive expertise.
key_points: China's manufacturing advantage over the U.S. is not just wages but vocational skills, workforce scale, and proximity to component suppliers
Apple's operational model deliberately avoids owning assembly facilities — when products decline, Foxconn bears the infrastructure burden, not Apple
The sheer scale of iPhone production (nearly one million per day) makes U.S. assembly practically impossible with current infrastructure
The only viable path to U.S. assembly would be full automation, which would create zero manufacturing jobs — defeating the political argument
NeXT's fully automated factory is a historical precedent, but it only produced about 50,000 computers total versus Apple's current volumes
money_quotes: There is no facility in the U.S. that can do that. There might not be anywhere in the world other than China that can operate at that sort of scale.
That's almost one million iPhones per day. 10 iPhones per second. Think about that.
Part of the marvel of Apple's operations is that they can assemble and sell an unfathomable number of devices but they're not on the hook for the assembly plants and facilities.
When iPhones go the way of the iPod in 10 or 15 or 20 years, Apple doesn't have any factories to close or convert for other uses. Foxconn does.
The only way Apple could ever switch to U.S. assembly and manufacturing would be if they automated the entire process — to build machines that build the machines.

So long as assembling these devices remains labor intensive, it has to happen in China. And if someday it becomes automated — if the machines are built by machines — by definition it's not going to create manufacturing jobs.
You can take every tool and die maker in the United States and probably put them in a room that we're currently sitting in. In China, you would have to have multiple football fields.
key_insight: Bringing Apple manufacturing to the U.S. is a false promise: the scale requires China's workforce, and the only alternative — full automation — would create zero jobs.
